States or within either of the Settlements under the law (including the provisions of any such Proclamation) in force in the said State or in the Straits Settlements before the appointed day, and such jurisdiction shall be exercised in conformity with the law in force in the State or Settlement concerned at the date when the offence was committed or the cause of action arose and in accordance, as nearly as may be, with the procedure in force in Courts constituted under this Order.

91. Military Courts established in the Malay States or the Settlements by the British Military Administration by virtue of any Proclamation made by, or under the authority of the Supreme Allied Commander, South East Asia, in which proceedings have been commenced and are pending at the date of this Order shall be deemed to be lawfully constituted Courts with jurisdiction to continue and conclude such proceedings and to issue warrants for the custody of prisoners and the carrying out of sentences in accordance with such Proclamation and any Rules, Regulations or Orders made thereunder, after the appointed day. The powers and functions of a confirming authority or reviewing authority under any such Proclamation may be exercised by such person or persons as the Governor shall designate in that behalf.

92.—(1) In this section—

(i) "Malay States property" means property which, immediately before the appointed day, was vested in or was in the possession, or under the control or held on account of His Majesty, or the Ruler of any of the Malay States, or the Government of the Federated Malay States, or the Government of any of the Malay States, or any officer of any such Government for any of the purposes of any such Government;

(ii) "Straits Settlements property" means property which, immediately before the appointed day, was vested in, or was in the possession, or under the control, or held on account of His Majesty, or the Government of the Straits Settlements, or any officer of that Government, for any of the purposes of that Government; and

(iii) Any reference to property vested in any person or authority shall be construed as including a reference to any property in the possession, or under the control, or held on account, of that person or authority; and any direction that property shall be vested in any person or authority shall likewise be construed as including a direction, as the case may require, that the possession or control of property shall be transferred to, or that property shall be held on account of, that person or authority.

(2) Save as in this section otherwise provided, all Malay States property shall, as from the appointed day, vest in His Majesty, or in the appropriate officer of the Government of the Union, or in the person or body prescribed under sub-section (7) of this section, as the case may be, for the purposes of the Government of the Union.

(3) Save as in this section otherwise provided, all Straits Settlements property which was, immediately before the appointed day, either—

(a) immovable property situate in the Settlements or either of them; or

(b) vested solely for a purpose of the Government of the Straits Settlements which will, after the appointed day, be a purpose of the [POSSIBLY MISSING]

===== Page 445 =====
Government of the Union in relation to the Settlements or either of them;

shall vest in His Majesty or the appropriate officer of the Government of the Union, or the person or body prescribed under sub-section (7) of this section, as the case may be, for the purposes of the Government of the Union.

(4) Save as in this section otherwise provided any Straits Settlements property which was, immediately before the appointed day, vested for any purpose of the Government of the Straits Settlements which was common to the Settlements or either of them and to any other territory formerly included in the Straits Settlements but not included in the Union, shall be apportioned between the Union and such territory by agreement between the Governor and the Governor of such territory with the approval of a Secretary of State; or, in default of such agreement, shall be apportioned by order of a Secretary of State. Any such agreement or order may contain provisions for the assessment of values, for the perfecting of any transfer of property mentioned therein and for financial and other adjustments, and may contain such other provisions as may be considered necessary or expedient for carrying the apportionment into effect.

(5) Any Straits Settlements property apportioned to the Union under sub-section (4) of this section shall be vested in His Majesty or the appropriate officer of the Government of the Union, or the person or body prescribed under sub-section (7) of this section, as the case may be, for the purposes of the Government of the Union; and the Governor may make such vesting or other orders as may be considered necessary or expedient for carrying the provisions of this Section into effect, and for such purpose may appoint and empower persons to transfer property.

(6) Notwithstanding the foregoing provisions of this section, any property which, immediately before the appointed day, was vested in the Ruler of any of the Malay States for any of the following purposes, shall continue to be vested in the Sultan of the State for the same purposes, that is to say—

(a) any property held by, or in trust for, or for the benefit of a Ruler for his official or personal use; or

(b) any building or land used for purposes of the Muhammadan religion, or property pertaining to the endowment of Muhammadan religious institutions;

and nothing in this section shall affect the use or enjoyment of any property vested in a Ruler or in His Majesty for any of the said purposes.

(7) The Governor may make regulations for determining, in cases of doubt, the person or body in whom property affected by this section shall be vested, and generally for giving effect to this section, and may, by Order, exclude any particular property from the operation of this section, or declare, for the removal of doubt, whether and in what manner, any particular property is affected thereby.

Transfer of liabilities.

93.—(1) All liabilities incurred by or on behalf of the Government of the Federated Malay States or of the Government of any of the Malay States for any of the purposes of any such Government shall be deemed to have been incurred by or on behalf of the Government of the Union.

===== Page 446 =====
(2) Without prejudice to the generality of the foregoing sub-section—

(a) Such liabilities shall include claims in respect of Loans issued, and Post Office Savings Bank deposits received, by the Government of the Federated Malay States or the Government of any of the Malay States.

(b) Claims to pensions, gratuities and other like allowances in respect of service under the Government of the Federated Malay States or the Government of any of the Malay States before the appointed day, whether arising before or after that day, shall be liabilities deemed to have been incurred by or on behalf of the Government of the Union.

(3) All liabilities incurred by or on behalf of the Government of the Straits Settlements solely for a purpose which will, after the appointed day, be a purpose of the Government of the Union in relation to the Settlements or either of them shall be deemed to have been incurred by or on behalf of the Government of the Union.

(4) It shall be lawful for the Governor, with the approval of a Secretary of State, to make, vary, rescind and carry into effect, arrangements with the Governor of any territory formerly included in the Straits Settlements but not included in the Union for the payment to the Government of that territory from the funds of the Government of the Union, of contribution towards the sums necessary to defray such of the liabilities of the Government of the Straits Settlements as shall have been transferred, by any Order in Council or otherwise, to the Government of that territory, including liabilities for the service and repayment of Loans and War Savings Certificates issued, Post Office Savings Bank Deposits received and pensions allowances and gratuities payable, by the Government of the Straits Settlements. Such contributions shall be proportionate to the share of such liabilities which, a Secretary of State is satisfied, is properly attributable to the Settlements.

(5) Claims in respect of liabilities deemed, by virtue of this section, to have been incurred by or on behalf of the Government of the Union which, if the Act of 1946 had not been passed and this Order had not been made, might have been enforced against His Majesty in His Government of the Straits Settlements, or against the Government of the Federated Malay States or of any of the Malay States or against any officer of any such Government in his official capacity or against any department of any such Government in connection with the administration of the Settlements or either of them or of the Federated Malay States or of any Malay State, may be enforced in such manner as may be prescribed by laws for the time being in force or by any regulation made by the Governor under sub-section (6) of this section, against His Majesty in His Government of the Union or against the Government or the appropriate officer of the Government of the Union, as the case may be.

(6) The Governor may make regulations for determining in any case of doubt the person or body by or against whom claims may be enforced under this section and generally for giving effect thereto.

94. The provisions of sections 88 to 93 inclusive of this Order may be amended by laws made under Part V or under section 85 of this Order.

===== Page 447 =====
PART X.

MISCELLANEOUS.

Oath of Allegiance.

95. Except for the purpose of enabling this section to be complied with, no Member shall sit or vote on any Council constituted under Section 13 and Parts IV and VII of this Order until he shall have taken and subscribed before the Governor, or some person authorised by the Governor in that behalf, the Oath of Allegiance in the form set out in the First Schedule to this Order:

Provided that every person authorised by law to make an affirmation instead of taking an oath in legal proceedings may make such affirmation in like terms instead of the said oath.

Provisions necessary to give effect to the Order.

96.—(1) Provision may be made by laws made under this Order and, until such provision has been so made, by regulations made by the Governor, regulating any matter which by this Order or by Instructions under His Majesty’s Sign Manual and Signet is to be prescribed.

(2) In particular and without prejudice to the generality of the foregoing sub-section, provision may be made regulating the election of Elected Members to any State Council or Settlement Council constituted by this Order including provision for regulating any of the following matters, that is to say:

(a) the persons, associations or bodies by whom Members are to be elected;
(b) the holding of elections;
(c) the qualifications of voters;
(d) the registration of voters;
(e) the ascertainment of the qualifications of voters and candidates;
(f) offences in relation to elections including the trial and punishment thereof; and
(g) the disposal of election petitions and the determination of questions otherwise arising regarding membership of the Councils.

(3) Such regulations shall be published in the Gazette; and thereafter every such regulation shall take effect on the day of publication or on such other day as may be therein specified and shall have the force of law.

Unqualified persons sitting or voting.

97. Any person who—

(a) having been elected or appointed a member of any Council constituted by this Order, but not having been at the time of such election or appointment, qualified to be so elected or appointed, shall sit or vote in such Council; or

(b) shall sit or vote in such Council after his seat therein has become vacant or he has become disqualified from sitting or voting therein; knowing, or having reasonable grounds for knowing, that he was disqualified, or that his seat has become vacant, as the case may be, shall be liable to a penalty not exceeding two hundred dollars for every day upon which he so sits or votes.

(2) The said penalty shall be recoverable by action in the Supreme Court at the suit of the Attorney General.

===== Page 448 =====
98. No Council constituted by this Order shall be disqualified from the transaction of business by reason of any vacancy among the Members, or by reason of the sitting or voting therein of any unqualified or disqualified person.

99. For the avoidance of doubts it is hereby declared that any law or regulation made under this Order may be made to operate retrospectively to any date.

100.—(1) If any difficulty shall arise in bringing into operation any of the provisions of this Order or in giving effect to the purposes thereof, a Secretary of State may, by Order, make such provision as seems to him necessary or expedient for the purpose of removing the difficulty and may by such Order amend or add to any provision of this Order:

Provided that no Order under this section shall be made later than the first day of January, 1948:

(2) Any Order under this section may be amended, added to, or revoked by a further Order, and may be given retrospective effect to a day not earlier than the appointed day.

101. Nothing in this Order shall affect the power of His Majesty in Council to make laws from time to time for the peace, order and good government of the Union.

FIRST SCHEDULE.
OATH OF ALLEGIANCE.
(Sections 6 and 95).

I, ... do swear that I will be faithful and bear true allegiance to His Majesty King George VI, His Heirs and Successors, according to Law. So help me God.

OATH FOR THE DUE EXECUTION
OF THE OFFICE OF GOVERNOR.

I, ... do swear that I will well and truly serve His Majesty King George VI, His Heirs and Successors, in the Office of Governor. So help me God.

THE SECOND SCHEDULE.
(Section 87 (3)).
Adaptation of Acts of Parliament.

12 & 13 Geo. V, c. 17.

The Pensions (Governors of Dominions, etc.) Act, 1929
19 Geo. V, c. 16.

Subsection (2) of section 45 of the Act shall have effect as if the words "Negri Sembilan, Pahang, Perak, Selangor" were substituted for the words "the Federated Malay States and" in line two.

Section 9 of the Act shall have effect as if the words "Malayan Union" were substituted for the words "Malay States" in the last line.

===== Page 449 =====
AT THE COURT AT BUCKINGHAM PALACE
The 27th day of March, 1946

Present

THE KING'S MOST EXCELLENT MAJESTY
IN COUNCIL.

WHEREAS there was this day read at the Board a letter to the Lord President of the Council from the Right Honourable George Henry Hall, one of His Majesty's Principal Secretaries of State, transmitting the Draft of Instructions to be given to the Governor and Commander-in-Chief of the Malayan Union:

His Majesty, having taken the same into consideration, was pleased, by and with the advice of His Privy Council, to approve thereof, and to order, as it is hereby ordered, that the Right Honourable George Henry Hall, one of His Majesty's Principal Secretaries of State, do prepare the said Instructions (which are hereunto annexed) for His Majesty's Royal Signature.

===== Page 450 =====
INSTRUCTIONS to Our Governor and Commander-in-Chief in and over the Malayan Union, or to any other officer for the time being administering the Government of the Malayan Union.

WHEREAS by the Malayan Union Order in Council, 1946 (hereinafter called "the Order in Council") We have ordered that as from the appointed day there shall be a Governor and Commander-in-Chief (in the Order in Council and hereinafter called "the Governor") in and over the Malayan Union (in the Order in Council and hereinafter called "the Union");

AND WHEREAS We have by the Order in Council empowered and commanded the Governor to do all things that belong to his said office in accordance with the Order in Council and such Commission as may be issued to him under Our Sign Manual and Signet and according to such Instructions as may from time to time be given to him under our Sign Manual and Signet, or through a Secretary of State, and such Orders in Council and other laws as may from time to time be in force in the Union;

AND WHEREAS We are minded to issue these Our Instructions under Our Sign Manual and Signet for the guidance of the Governor and any other Officer who may administer the Government of the Union;

NOW, THEREFORE, as from the appointed day, We do hereby direct and enjoin and declare Our Will and pleasure as follows:

1. The Governor may, whenever he thinks fit, require any person in the public service of the Union to take the Oath of Allegiance in the form prescribed by the Order in Council, together with such other oath or oaths as may from time to time be prescribed by any laws in force in the Union. The Governor is to administer such oaths or cause them to be administered by some public officer of the Union.

2. Whenever there is a subsisting appointment of a Deputy to the Governor under the Order in Council, these Instructions, so far as they apply to any matter or thing to be done, or any functions to be performed by such Deputy, shall be deemed to be addressed to and shall be observed by such Deputy.

3. The Executive Council of the Union shall consist of the Governor as Chairman, three ex officio members, that is to say the Chief Secretary, the Attorney General and the Financial Secretary, together with two members holding office of emolument under the Crown in the Union (hereinafter called "Official Members") and five members, not holding such office (hereinafter called "Unofficial Members") to be appointed by Us or by the Governor by Instrument under the Public Seal of the Union in pursuance of instructions from Us through a Secretary of State.

===== Page 451 =====
4.—(1) Subject to the provisions of the Order in Council, every Official or Unofficial Member of the Executive Council shall hold his seat in Council during His Majesty's pleasure, and, subject thereto, shall cease to be a Member at the end of three years from the date of the Instrument by which he is appointed, or such earlier date as may be provided by such Instrument; or previously if his seat shall become vacant under the provisions of these Instructions.

If any person is appointed to be a Temporary Member of the Council and his temporary appointment is immediately followed by his definitive appointment, the said period of three years shall be reckoned from the date of the Instrument by which he is appointed a Temporary Member.

(2) The seat of a Member of the Executive Council shall become vacant—
(a) upon his death; or
(b) if, being an Official Member, he shall cease to hold office of emolument under the Crown in the Union; or
(c) if, being an Unofficial Member, he shall be appointed permanently to any such office; or
(d) if he shall, by writing under his hand addressed to the Governor, resign his seat in the Executive Council; or
(e) if he shall be absent from the Union without written permission from the Governor:

Provided that a Member shall not be deemed to be absent from the Union for the purpose of this paragraph by reason only of absence in the Colony of Singapore for a period not exceeding one month.

(3) Any person vacating a seat as a Member of the Executive Council may, if qualified, be again appointed from time to time.

(4) If an Unofficial Member be appointed temporarily to any office of emolument under the Crown in the Union or to act in any such office, he shall not sit as an Unofficial Member of the Executive Council so long as he continues to hold or act in that office.

(5) The Governor may, by Instrument under the Public Seal, suspend any Official or Unofficial Member from the exercise of his functions as a Member of the Executive Council. Every such suspension shall forthwith be reported by the Governor to Us through a Secretary of State and shall remain in force unless and until it shall be removed by the Governor by Instrument under the Public Seal, or by Us through a Secretary of State, or the person suspended ceases to be a Member of the Executive Council.

(6) The Governor may, by Instrument under the Public Seal, declare any Official or Unofficial Member to be incapable of discharging his functions as a Member of the Council, and thereupon such Member shall not sit or vote in the Council until he is declared, in manner aforesaid, to be again capable of discharging his said functions.

5.—(1) Whenever there shall be a vacancy in the number of persons sitting in the Executive Council by reason of the fact that—
(a) an ex officio or Official Member is administering the Government of the Union; or
(b) one person is lawfully discharging the functions of more than one of the three officers specified in clause 3 of these Instructions; or

===== Page 452 =====
(c) No person is lawfully discharging the functions of one of those officers; or
(d) a Member is suspended from the exercise of his functions as a Member; or
(e) the seat of a Member is vacant for any cause; or
(f) a Member is unable to sit or vote in the Council in consequence of a declaration by the Governor, as provided in these Instructions, that he is incapable of discharging his functions as a Member; or
(g) a Member is absent from the Union; or
(h) an Unofficial Member has been appointed temporarily to an office of emolument under the Crown in the Union or to act in any such office;

a person may be appointed a Temporary Member for the period of such vacancy.

(2) The said person shall be appointed by the Governor by Instrument under the Public Seal and—
(a) in the case of a vacancy in the number of persons sitting in the Executive Council as ex officio or Official Members, shall be a person holding office of emolument under the Crown in the Union; and
(b) in the case of a vacancy in the number of persons sitting in the Executive Council as Unofficial Members, shall be a person not holding any such office.

(3) Every person so appointed shall, as long as his appointment shall subsist, be to all intents and purposes—
(a) in the case of a vacancy in the number of Members sitting in the Executive Council as ex officio or Official Members, an Official Member; and
(b) in the case of a vacancy in the number of persons sitting in the Executive Council as Unofficial Members, an Unofficial Member; and, subject to the provisions of this clause, the provisions of clause 4 of these Instructions shall apply accordingly: Provided that, in the case of a vacancy in the number of persons sitting in the Council as ex officio Members, the person so appointed shall not be regarded as an Official Member in computing the number of such Members for purposes of clause 3 of these Instructions.

(4) The Governor shall forthwith report every such temporary appointment to Us through a Secretary of State. Any such appointment may (without prejudice to anything done by virtue thereof) be disallowed by Us through a Secretary of State or revoked by the Governor by Instrument under the Public Seal.

(5) A temporary appointment shall cease to have effect on notification by the Governor to the person appointed of disallowance by Us or of revocation by the Governor, or on supersession of the appointment by the definite appointment of a person to fill the vacancy, or when the vacancy shall otherwise cease to exist.

6. The Members of the Executive Council shall take precedence as We may specially assign and, in the absence of such assignment, as follows:

First, the ex officio Members in the order in which they are specified in clause 3 of these Instructions;

===== Page 453 =====
Secondly, the Official Members, according to the priority of their appointments as Official Members; or if appointed on the same day, in such order as may be assigned by the Governor;

Thirdly, the Official Members temporarily appointed under clause 5, in such order as may be assigned by the Governor;

Fourthly, the Unofficial Members in order according to the dates when they were first appointed to be Members, or temporary Members as the case may be, of the Executive Council, and, in the case of more than one appointment on the same day, in such order as may be assigned by the Governor or, in the absence of such assignment, according to the alphabetical order of their names:

Provided that, where a Member is reappointed to fill a vacancy caused by the termination of his own term of office, the date from which he has been continuously a Member of the Council (any interval between such termination and re-appointment being disregarded) shall be regarded as the date of his appointment for the purposes of this clause.

7.—(1) The Executive Council shall not be summoned except by the authority of the Governor.

(2) The Executive Council shall not be disqualified for the transaction of business by reason of any vacancy among the Members; but no business except that of adjournment shall be transacted if objection is taken by any Member present that there are less than three Members present besides the Governor or Member presiding.

8. The Governor shall, so far as is practicable, attend and preside at all meetings of the Executive Council, and, in his absence, such member as the Governor may appoint or, in the absence of such Member or if no Member be so appointed, the senior Member of the Council present shall preside.

9. The Governor shall consult with the Executive Council in the execution of all powers and authorities conferred upon him, except in cases—

(a) which are of such a nature that, in his judgment, Our service would sustain material prejudice by consulting the Council thereupon; or

(b) in which the matters to be decided shall be too unimportant to require their advice; or

(c) in which the matters are too urgent to admit of their advice being given by the time within which it may be necessary for him to act.

In every case falling within paragraph (c) of this clause, he shall, as soon as practicable, communicate to the Council the measures which he shall have adopted with the reasons therefor.

10. The Governor shall alone be entitled to submit questions to the Executive Council; but if the Governor decline to submit any question to the Council when requested in writing by any Member so to do, it shall be competent to such Member to require that there be recorded upon the Minutes his written application together with the answer returned by the Governor thereto.

11. The Governor may act in opposition to the advice given to him by Members of the Executive Council, if in any case it shall in his judgment be right so to do; but in any such case he shall at the first convenient opportunity, fully report the matter to Us through a Secretary of State with the reasons for his action. In every such case it shall

Summoning and quorum.

Governor to preside.

Consultation with Executive Council.

Governor to propose questions.

Governor may act in opposition to Executive Council.

===== Page 454 =====
be competent to any Member of the Council to require that there be recorded upon the Minutes any advice or opinion he may give upon the question and the grounds therefor.

12. The Governor shall forthwith communicate to the Executive Council these Our Instructions and all such others as from time to time We may direct, or he shall find convenient for Our service to impart to them.

13. Minutes shall be kept of all the proceedings of the Executive Council and the Legislative Council. At every meeting of each Council, the Minutes of the last preceding meeting shall be confirmed, with or without amendment as the case may require, before proceeding to the despatch of any other business. Twice in each year, a full transcript of all Minutes of the Executive Council and of the Legislative Council for the preceding half-year shall be transmitted to Us through a Secretary of State.

14. (1) In the making of laws, the Governor and the Legislative Council shall observe, as far as practicable, the following Rules:

(a) All laws shall be styled "Ordinances" and the words of enactment shall be "enacted by the Governor of the Malayan Union, with the advice and consent of the Legislative Council": Provided that in the case of:

(i) any law made by the Governor under section 85 of the Order in Council, the words of enactment shall be "enacted by the Governor of the Malayan Union in accordance with the provisions of section 85 of the Malayan Union Order in Council, 1946"; and

(ii) in the case of any law having effect by virtue of a declaration made by the Governor under section 29 of the Order in Council, the words of enactment shall be "enacted by the Governor in accordance with the provisions of section 29 of the Malayan Union Order in Council, 1946".

(b) All Ordinances shall be distinguished by titles and shall be divided into successive sections consecutively numbered, and to every section there shall be annexed in the margin a short indication of its contents. The Ordinances enacted in each year shall be distinguished by consecutive numbers, commencing in each year with the number one.

(c) Each different matter shall be provided for by a different Ordinance without inter-mixing in one and the same Ordinance such things as have no proper relation to each other. No provision shall be inserted in any Ordinance which shall be foreign to what the title of such Ordinance imports; and no permanent provision shall be included in any temporary Ordinance.

(d) In the making of laws Resident Commissioners and State Councils and Settlement Councils shall observe, as far as practicable, the following Rules:

(a) All laws shall be styled "Enactments" and the words of enactment shall be "enacted by the Governor with the advice and consent of the State Council"; or "enacted by the Governor with the advice and consent of the Settlement Council", as the case may be.

===== Page 455 =====
(b) The provisions of sub-paragraphs (b) and (c) of paragraph (1) of this clause shall apply to Enactments of State Councils and Settlement Councils.

(3) In the making of laws, Sultans in Sultan's Advisory Councils shall conform, as nearly as may be, to any Instructions given by the Governor under the Public Seal in that behalf.

15.—(1) Save as hereinafter mentioned, the Governor shall not, without having previously obtained instructions through a Secretary of State, assent to any Bill passed by the Legislative Council, which falls within any of the following classes, unless such Bill contains a clause suspending the operation thereof until the signification in the Union of Our pleasure thereon, that is to say:

(1) Any Bill for the divorce of married persons;
(2) Any Bill whereby any grant of land or money, or other donation or gratuity, may be made to himself;
(3) Any Bill affecting the currency of the Union, or relating to the issue of bank notes;
(4) Any Bill establishing any Banking Association, or amending or altering the constitution, powers, or privileges of any Banking Association;
(5) Any Bill imposing differential duties;
(6) Any Bill the provisions of which shall appear to him to be inconsistent with obligations imposed upon Us by Treaty;
(7) Any Bill interfering with the discipline or control of Our Forces by land or sea or air;
(8) Any Bill relating to questions of defence or any matter affecting naval, military, or air forces or the control of aerial navigation or aircraft or the transport or means of communication of naval, military or air forces;
(9) Any Bill of any extraordinary nature and importance whereby Our prerogative, or the rights and property of Our subjects not residing in the Union, or the trade, transport or communications of any part of Our dominions, may be prejudiced;
(10) Any Bill containing any provision prohibiting, regulating or licensing the import or export of goods, or imposing or varying any customs, excise or export duty, tariff, levy or cess, or allowing any discount, rebate or drawback from any such duty, tariff, levy, or cess, or containing any other provision (whether of a similar nature to the foregoing or not), whereby the trade or commerce of the Colony of Singapore might be prejudicially affected, unless the Governor of the said Colony, having previously been informed of the provision, shall have signified that he does not object thereto;
(11) Any Bill whereby persons of any racial, religious or other community are made liable to any disabilities or restrictions to which persons of other such communities are not also subjected or made liable, or are granted advantages not extended to persons of other such communities;
(12) Any Bill the principle of which has evoked serious opposition by any racial, religious, or other community and which in the opinion of the Governor is likely to involve oppression or unfairness to any such community;
(13) Any Bill containing provisions to which Our assent has been once refused, or which have been disallowed by Us:

===== Page 456 =====
Provided that the Governor may assent in Our name to any Bill falling within any of the classes described in this sub-clause (except a Bill falling within any class mentioned in paragraph (6) or (10) of this sub-clause), if he shall have satisfied himself that an urgent necessity exists requiring that such Bill be brought into immediate operation but, in any such case, he shall at the earliest opportunity, transmit to Us any Bill to which he shall have assented, together with his reasons for so assenting.

(2) The Governor shall not, without previously having obtained instructions from a Secretary of State, permit to be introduced or put into operation by subordinate legislation or executive action any such provision as is mentioned in paragraph (10) of sub-clause (1) of this clause, unless the Governor of the Colony of Singapore, having previously been informed of the provision, shall have signified that he does not object thereto.

Private Bills.

16.—(1) Every Bill (not being a Government measure) intended to affect or benefit some particular person, association, or corporate body, shall contain a clause saving the right of Us, Our Heirs and Successors, all bodies politic and corporate, and all others, except such as are mentioned in the Bill and those claiming by, from, or under them.

(2) No such Bill shall be introduced into the Legislative Council until due notice has been given by not less than three successive publications of the Bill in the Gazette of the Union, and the Governor shall not assent thereto in Our Name unless it has been so published. A certificate under the hand of the Governor signifying that such publication has been made shall be transmitted to Us with the Bill or Ordinance.

Laws made by the Governor.

17. The provisions of clauses 15 and 16 shall apply mutatis mutandis to any law made by the Governor under section 85 of the Order in Council as they apply to a Bill requiring the Assent of the Governor.

18. When any Ordinance of the Legislative Council or Enactment of a State or Settlement Council shall have been enacted, the Governor shall forthwith transmit to Us, through a Secretary of State, for the signification of our pleasure, a transcript in duplicate of the Ordinance or Enactment duly authenticated under the Public Seal of the Union and by his own signature, together with an explanation of the reasons and occasion for the enactment of the Ordinance or Enactment.

Laws to be published.

19. As soon as practicable after the commencement of each year, the Governor shall cause a complete collection of all Ordinances and Enactments enacted during the preceding year to be published for general information.

Appointments.

20.—(1) Every appointment by the Governor of any person to any office or employment shall, unless otherwise provided by law, be expressed to be during pleasure only.

(2) Save with the consent of the Governor, no person shall be appointed to public office in the Union who is not a Malayan Union citizen.

Regulations of power of pardon in capital cases.

21.—(1) Whenever any offender shall have been condemned by sentence of any civil Court in the Union to suffer death, the Governor shall call upon the Judge who presided at the trial to make to him a written report of the case of such offender, and shall cause such report to be taken into consideration at a meeting of the Executive Council, and he may cause the said Judge to be specially summoned to attend at such meeting and to produce his notes thereat. The Governor shall not pardon or reprieve any such offender unless it shall appear to

===== Page 457 =====
him expedient so to do, upon receiving the advice of the Executive Council thereon; but in all such cases he is to decide either to extend or to withhold a pardon or reprieve, according to his own deliberate judgment, whether the Members of the Executive Council concur therein or otherwise, entering nevertheless, in the Minutes of the Executive Council, his reasons at length, in case he should decide any such question in opposition to the judgment of the majority of the Members thereof.

(2) Until the Executive Council shall be constituted, references in this clause to the Executive Council shall be construed as references to the Advisory Council.

22. The Governor shall forward to Us, each year, through a Secretary of State, the annual book of returns commonly called the Blue Book, relating to Revenue and Expenditure, Public Works, Legislation, Civil Establishments, Pensions, Population, Schools, Course of Exchange, Imports and Exports, Agricultural Produce, Manufactures, and other matters in the said Blue Book more particularly specified with reference to the state and condition of the Union.

23. Except in circumstances in which he is not regarded as absent from the Union for the purposes of the Order in Council, the Governor shall not quit the Union without having first obtained leave from Us for so doing through a Secretary of State.

24.—(1) In these Our Instructions, unless inconsistent with the context:

“the appointed day” has the same meaning as in the Order in Council;

“the Executive Council”, “the Legislative Council” and “the Advisory Council” mean respectively the Executive Council, the Legislative Council and the Advisory Council of the Union constituted under and by virtue of the Order in Council;

“the Governor” includes every person for the time being administering the Government of the Union;

“Malayan Union citizen” means a citizen of the Union within the meaning of any Order in Council providing for Malayan Union citizenship;

“State Councils”, “Settlement Councils” and “Sultan’s Advisory Councils” mean respectively the State Councils, Settlement Councils and Sultan’s Advisory Councils constituted under and by virtue of the Order in Council; and

“Secretary of State” means one of Our Principal Secretaries of State.

(2) For the purposes of these Our Instructions, a person shall not be deemed to hold an office of emolument under the Crown in the Union by reason only that he is in receipt of a pension or other like allowance in respect of service under the Crown.

If it shall be declared by any law for the time being in force in the Union that an office shall not be an office of emolument under the Crown in the Union for all or any of the purposes of these Our Instructions, these Instructions shall have effect accordingly as if such law were enacted therein.

Given at Our Court at Saint James’s, this 27th day of March, 1946, in the Tenth year of Our Reign.

Printed by His Majesty’s Stationery Office Press, Drury Lane, W.C.2.

Dear Leadbitter,

It is imperative that all the constitutional documents relating to the establishment of Civil Government in the Malayan Union and Singapore should be passed by the Privy Council in time to enable all subsequent formalities to be completed so that the instruments can be brought into operation locally on the 1st April, the date on which the military authorities are to hand over.

The Straits Settlements (Repeal) Bill upon which these Instruments will be largely dependent is still before Parliament, but we anticipate that it will pass its remaining stages during the next week or so. We are hoping that Royal Assent will be given on the 26th March.

This is running matters rather close, and we should be grateful therefore if you would arrange to convene a meeting of the Council on that date after the Royal Assent has been given. We will confirm, as soon as possible, that that date stands.

So far as we can see at present the instruments to be submitted to the Council are as follows:

Straits Settlements.

An Order in Council fixing the "appointed day" under the Act:

Singapore Colony.

Constitution Order in Council.
Royal Instructions.

Malayan Union.

Constitution Order in Council.
Royal Instructions.
Citizenship Order in Council.

Sir Eric Leadbitter, C.V.O.

North Borneo (Not certain).

Letters Patent.
Royal Instructions.

Yours sincerely,

K. O. ROBERTS-WRAY.

Dear Leadbitter,

We are anxious that all the constitutional documents relating to the establishment of Civil Government in the Malayan Union and Singapore shall be passed by the Privy Council before the 1st April next, the date on which this has been fixed to take place.

===== Page 467 =====

This, as you will realise, is running matters rather close, and we should be grateful therefore if you would arrange to convene a meeting of the Council on that date. We will confirm, as soon as possible, that that date stands.

PART VIII

93. Operation of Part VIII. Original.

94. Governor may make laws. cf. A.12. Note: This empowers the Governor to legislate with the advice of an Advisory Council. To resolve doubts, it is desirable to give the Governor express powers to legislate retrospectively to any date prior to the appointed day. For instance, there is a gap between the lapsing of the Emergency Powers Acts and, with them, the Straits Settlements Defence Regulations, and the date when a local Ordinance can enact similar provisions. This may require coverage. Also, the Indemnity Ordinance and other Ordinances will or may require to be given retrospective effect.

95. Advisory Council. Original.

96. Continuance of Existing Laws. Largely original, based on A.16 and Dom. 36. See S.2 definition of "Existing Laws".

(2) Please consider.

(3) The Second Schedule is not yet complete.

97. Validity of previous acts. Dom. 37. A.17. adapted.

98. (1) Pending proceedings. A.18. Dom. 38. adapted.

===== Page 477 =====
(2) Limitation of actions

c.f. T. 21 (3)
A provision on these lines will, I think, be essential. Consider and discuss.

99. Crimes committed and causes of action arising prior to the appointed day.

c.f. T. 20. It will, I suggest, be desirable to resolve doubts by stating in advance that the Supreme Court of the Union has jurisdiction over offences and causes of action committed against, or arising under the laws of the Federated Malay States, the Unfederated Malay States, and Straits Settlements prior to the appointed day. An offence committed or cause of action arising prior to the appointed day might not be discovered, or an offender might not be apprehended, until after the appointed day. There must be jurisdiction to deal with such cases and it is better to confer it expressly and to state the law and procedure to be applied.

100. Military Courts.

c.f. T. 21 (1) and (2). This will avoid lengthy re-hearing of cases and release of under trial prisoners.

101. Transfer of property.

Based on Dom. 39 with substantial variations. Please consider.

102. (1) Transfer of liabilities

Original for the most part, but based on Dom. 40 and 41 and A.19. S.101 deals with transfer of property, this section with transfer of liabilities.

Query. What is the policy regarding liabilities of the B.M.A.?

THE MALAYAN UNION

THE MALAYAN UNION ORDER IN COUNCIL, 1946.

At the Court at
The day of 194.

PRESENT

The King's Most Excellent Majesty in Council.

WHEREAS by the Foreign Jurisdiction Act, 1890, it is among other things enacted that it shall be lawful for His Majesty the King to hold, exercise, and enjoy any jurisdiction which His Majesty now has or may at any time thereafter have within a foreign country in the same and as ample a manner as if His Majesty had acquired that jurisdiction by the cession or conquest of territory:

AND WHEREAS by treaty, grant, usage, sufferance, and other lawful means His Majesty has full power and jurisdiction within the Malay States of Johore, Kedah, Kelantan, Negri Sembilan, Pahang, Perak, Perlis, Selangor, and Trengganu (hereinafter referred to together as "the Malay States"):

AND WHEREAS, by the Straits Settlements (Repeal) Act, 1946 (hereinafter called "the Act of 1946") it is, among other things, enacted that on such day as His Majesty may by Order in Council appoint (in that Act and in this Order referred to as "the appointed day") the Straits Settlements Act, 1866, shall be repealed and the Straits Settlements, that is to say the settlements and dependencies mentioned in the Schedule to the Act of 1946 (which include, among other settlements and dependencies, the Settlement of Malacca and the Settlement of Penang) and their dependencies shall cease to be a single colony and that on and after the appointed day each of the said settlements and dependencies shall be divided into such territories as His Majesty may by Order in Council direct and that those territories shall be governed either singly or in conjunction with such other territories (whether falling within the Schedule to the Act of 1946 or not) as may be specified in such Order and that

===== Page 479 =====
Such Order in Council may contain such other and consequential provisions as are contained in this Order:

AND WHEREAS by the Act of 1946 it is further enacted that the British Settlements Acts, 1887 and 1945 shall apply in relation to each of the territories into which the said Settlements and dependencies are divided by Order under this Act as if it were a British settlement as so defined:

AND WHEREAS by the Straits Settlements Order in Council, 1946 it is provided that the appointed day shall be the day of 1944:

AND WHEREAS it is expedient to make provision for the administration, on and after the appointed day, of the Malay States in conjunction with the Settlement of Malacca and the Settlement of Penang and the dependencies of the said settlements.

NOW THEREFORE, His Majesty, by virtue and in exercise of the powers by the Foreign Jurisdiction Act, 1890 and by the Act of 1946 and the British Settlements Acts, 1887 and 1945, or otherwise in His Majesty vested, is pleased, by and with the advice of His Privy Council to order, and it is hereby ordered, as follows:

PART I - PRELIMINARY.

(1) This Order may be cited as the Malayan Union Order in Council, 1946 and, except as to section 15 and Parts II, III, IV, V, and VI, shall come into operation on the appointed day.

(2) Section 15 and Parts II, III, IV, V and VI of this Order shall come into operation on such day, or days respectively, subsequent to the appointed day, as may

===== Page 480 =====
THE MALAYAN UNION

THE MALAYAN UNION ORDER IN COUNCIL, 1946.

At the Court at
The day of 1946.

PRESENT

The King's Most Excellent Majesty in Council.

Whereas by the Foreign Jurisdiction Act, 1890 it is, among other things, enacted that it shall be lawful for His Majesty the King to hold, exercise and enjoy any jurisdiction which His Majesty now has or may at any time hereafter have within a foreign country in the same and as simple a manner as if His Majesty had acquired that jurisdiction by the cession or conquest of territory:

And Whereas by treaty, grant, usage, sufferance and other lawful means His Majesty has full power and jurisdiction within the Malay States of Johore, Kedah, Kelantan, Negri Sembilan, Pahang, Perak, Perlis, Selangor and Trengganu (hereinafter referred to together as "the Malay States"):

And Whereas, by the Straits Settlements (Repeal) Act, 1946 (hereinafter called "the Act of 1946") it is, among other things, enacted that on such day as His Majesty may by Order in Council appoint (in that Act and in this Order referred to as "the appointed day") the Straits Settlements Act, 1866, shall be repealed and the Straits Settlements, that is to say the territories mentioned in the Schedule to the Act of 1946 (which territories include, among other territories, the Settlement of Malacca and the Settlement of Penang), shall cease to be a single colony and that on and after the appointed day each of the said territories shall be governed as His Majesty may by Order in Council direct either singly or in conjunction with such other territories (whether mentioned in the Schedule to the Act of 1946 or not) as may be specified in such Order.

===== Page 481 =====
And by the Act of 1946 His Majesty in Council is empowered to make such other and consequential provisions as are contained in this Order:

And Whereas by the Act of 1946 it is further enacted that the British Settlements Acts, 1887 and 1945 (which authorize His Majesty to make laws and establish institutions for British settlements as defined in those Acts) shall apply in relation to each of the said territories as if it were a British settlement as so defined; so however that no instrument made under the British Settlements Acts, 1887 and 1945 relating to any of the said territories shall come into operation before the appointed day:

And Whereas by the Straits Settlements Order in Council, 1946 it is provided that the appointed day shall be the day of 1944:

And Whereas it is expedient to make provision for the administration, on and after the appointed day, of the Malay States in conjunction with the Settlement of Malacca and the Settlement of Penang:

Now Therefore, His Majesty, by virtue and in exercise of the powers by the Foreign Jurisdiction Act, 1890 and by the Act of 1946 and the British Settlements Acts, 1887 and 1945 vested in His Majesty, is pleased, by and with the advice of His Privy Council to order, and it is hereby ordered, as follows:

PART I - PRELIMINARY.

(1) This Order may be cited as the Malayan Union Order in Council, 1946 and, except as to section 15 and Parts II, III, IV, V, and VI, shall come into operation on the appointed day.

(2) Section 15 and Parts II, III, IV, V AND VI of this Order shall come into operation on such day, or days respectively, subsequent to the appointed day, as may be

===== Page 482 =====
"The existing laws" means all Acts of Parliament, Orders of His Majesty in Council, Enactments of the Legislature of the Federated Malay States, Enactments of the Legislature of any Malay State, Ordinances of the Legislature of the Colony of the Straits Settlements, Proclamations issued by the High Commissioner of the Malay States or by the Ruler of any of the Malay States, or by the Governor of the Straits Settlements, or by, or under the authority of, the Supreme Allied Commander South East Asia Command, and all Rules, Regulations, By-laws and other enactments or instruments having the force of law in the Union or in any part thereof immediately prior to the appointed day, whether the same were administered by the British Military Administration or not.

===== Page 483 =====
fixed by the Governor by proclamation in the Gazette.

Interpretation. 2. In this Order, unless the context otherwise requires -

"the Chief Justice" means the Chief Justice of the Union;

"the Gazette" means the official Gazette of the Union;

"the Governor" means the Governor and Commander-in-Chief for the time being of the Union and includes the officer for the time being administering the government thereof, and, to the extent that a Deputy to the Governor is authorised to act that Deputy;

"the Governor in Council" means the Governor acting after consultation with, but not necessarily in accordance with the advice of, the Executive Council of the Union, not necessarily in such Council assembled; and, while Part VII of this Order is in operation, means the Governor;

"Malayan Union citizen" means a citizen of the Union within the meaning of the Malayan Union (Citizenship) Order in Council, 1946;

"property" includes all property movable or immovable, and all estates, interests, easements and rights whether equitable or legal, in, to or out of property, and things in action; and "vested" in relation to property includes rights to property which are contingent rights in remainder;

"the Public Seal" means the public seal of the Union;

"session" means the sittings of any Council constituted under this Order commencing when the Council first meets after being so constituted, or after its prorogation or dissolution at any time, and terminating when the Council is prorogued or is dissolved without having been prorogued;

===== Page 484 =====
"the Settlement of Malacca" means the Town and Fort and Province of Malacca and all islands and places heretofore known or administered as part of the Settlement of Malacca, and the territorial waters adjacent thereto;

"the Settlement of Penang" means Georgetown and the Island of Penang (or Prince of Water Island), Province Wellesley and all islands and places heretofore known or administered as part of the Settlement of Penang, and the territorial waters adjacent thereto;

"sitting" means any sitting or sittings of a Council constituted under this Order commencing when the Council first meets after being summoned at any time and terminating when the Council is adjourned sine die or at the conclusion of a session without adjournment;

"Sultan" includes Raja of Kelantan, the Raja of Perlis and the Yang di-Pertuan Besar of Negri Sembilan, and, during a Regency in any of the Malay States, means the Regent or, if there be more than one Regent, the Regent to whom is assigned the function in connection with which the term is used;

"the Supreme Court" means the Supreme Court of the Union;

"the Union" means the Malayan Union constituted by section 3 of this Order.

(2) For the purposes of this Order, when reference is made to a Sultan of any of the Malay States or to any public officer by the term designating his office, such reference means the Sultan for the time being of the State concerned, or the officer for the time being discharging the functions of that office, as the case may be.

(3) For the purposes of this Order a person shall not be deemed to hold an office of emolument under the Crown in

===== Page 485 =====
The Union by reason only that he is in receipt of a pension or other like allowance in respect of service under the Crown.

If it shall be declared by any law for the time being in force in the Union that the office shall not be an office of emolument under the Crown in the Union for all or any of the purposes of this Order, this Order shall have effect accordingly as if that law were enacted therein.

(4) The Interpretation Act, 1889 shall apply for the interpretation of this Order as it applies for the interpretation of any Act of Parliament.

3. There shall be established a Union comprising the Malay States and the Settlement of Malacca and the Settlement of Penang, to be governed and administered together and to be known as the Malayan Union.

PART II. THE GOVERNOR AND EXECUTIVE COUNCIL.

Governor.

4. There shall be a Governor and Commander-in-Chief (hereinafter called "the Governor") in and over the Union, and appointments to the said office shall be made by Commission under His Majesty's Sign Manual and Signet.

Powers of Governor.

5. The Governor is hereby empowered and commanded to do all things that belong to his said office in accordance with this Order and such Commission as may be issued to him under His Majesty's Sign Manual and Signet and according to such Instructions as may from time to time be given to him under His Majesty's Sign Manual and Signet, or through a Secretary of State, and such Orders in Council and other laws as may from time to time be in force in the Union.

Publication of Governor's Commission and taking of oaths.

6. Every person appointed to fill the office of Governor shall, before entering on any of the duties of his office, cause the Commission appointing him to be Governor to be read and published at the seat of Government, in the presence of the Chief Justice or, in his absence, of some other Judge.

===== Page 486 =====
of the Supreme Court, and of such members of the Executive Council of the Union as can conveniently attend; and shall take before them the Oath of Allegiance and the Oath for the due execution of the office of Governor in the forms set out in the First Schedule to this Order, which Oaths the Chief Justice or such other Judge as aforesaid shall administer.

7. (1) Whenever the office of Governor becomes vacant or the Governor is absent from the Union and there is no subsisting appointment of a Deputy under the next succeeding section of this Order, or the Governor is from any cause prevented from, or incapable of, performing the duties of his Office, then such person or persons as His Majesty may appoint under His Sign Manual and Signet or if there is no such person or persons in the Union so appointed and capable of discharging the duties of administration, the senior Member of the Executive Council then in the Union and so capable, shall, during His Majesty's pleasure, administer the Government of the Union, first taking, in manner in this Order prescribed, the oaths directed by this Order to be taken by the Governor. Thereupon such person or Member is hereby empowered and commanded, subject, if he is so appointed as aforesaid, to the terms of his appointment, to do, during His Majesty's pleasure, all things that belong to the Office of Governor as provided in this Order:

Provided that the Governor or any other person or Member as aforesaid shall not be regarded as absent from the Union for the purposes of this Order during his passage from one part of the Union to another or during a visit to any of the neighbouring territories in the exercise or discharge of any powers or duties conferred or imposed upon him by virtue of his office or when there is a

===== Page 487 =====
Subsisting appointment of a Deputy under the next succeeding section of this Order.

(2) Any such person as aforesaid shall not continue to administer the Government after the Governor or some other person having a prior right to administer the same has notified that he is about to assume or resume the administration.

8. Whenever the Governor has occasion to be absent from the seat of Government but not from the Union or to be absent from the Union for a period which he has reason to believe will be of short duration or whenever by reason of illness which he has reason to believe will be of short duration he considers it desirable so to do, he may, by Instrument under the Public Seal, appoint any person resident in the Union to be his Deputy during such absence or illness, and in that capacity to exercise and perform for and on behalf of the Governor during such absence or illness all such functions of the Governor as shall be specified.

(2) By the appointment of a Deputy as aforesaid the power and authority of the Governor shall not be abridged, altered, or in any way affected otherwise than as His Majesty may at any time hereafter think proper to direct, and every such Deputy shall conform to and observe all such instructions as the Governor shall from time to time address to him for his guidance.

(3) Any appointment under this section may at any time be revoked by the Governor or by a Secretary of State and in case of absence as aforesaid, shall cease and determine upon the return of the Governor to the seat of Government or to the Union, as the case may be.

===== Page 488 =====
9. The Governor shall keep and use the Public Seal of the Union for sealing all things whatsoever that shall pass the said Seal. Until a Public Seal shall be provided for the Union, an official stamp bearing the inscription "Malayan Union, Public Seal" may be used as the Public Seal.

10. Subject to the provisions of any law for the time being in force in the Union and of any Instructions from time to time given to the Governor under His Majesty's Sign Manual and Signet or through a Secretary of State, the Governor, in His Majesty's name and on His Majesty's behalf, may make and execute, under the Public Seal, grants and dispositions of any lands within the Colony which may be lawfully granted or disposed of by His Majesty.

11. Subject to the provisions of any law for the time being in force in the Union and to any Instructions from time to time given to him by His Majesty through a Secretary of State, the Governor, for cause shown to his satisfaction, may dismiss or suspend from the exercise of his office any person holding any public office within the Union, or may take such other disciplinary action as may seem to him desirable.

12. When any offence has been committed for which the offender may be tried within the Union, the Governor may, as he shall see occasion, in His Majesty's name and on His Majesty's behalf, grant a pardon to any accomplice in such offence who shall give such information as shall lead to the conviction of the principal offender or any one of such offenders if more than one; and further may grant to any offender convicted of any such offence in any Court within the Union, a pardon either free or subject to lawful conditions, or any respite either indefinite or for such period as the Governor may see fit, of the execution of any sentence passed on such offender, and may remit the whole

===== Page 489 =====
13. (1) There shall be in and for the Colony a Court of unlimited civil and criminal jurisdiction to be called the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court shall be a Court of Record and may consist of a High Court and a Court of Appeal.

(2) There shall be a Chief Justice appointed by His Majesty by Letters Patent, or by the Governor by Letters Patent under the Public Seal in accordance with such instructions as he may receive from His Majesty through a Secretary of State, and as many Judges of the Supreme Court, similarly appointed by the Governor, as may from time to time be required. The Chief Justice and Judges of the Supreme Court shall hold office during His Majesty's pleasure.

(3) The constitution, powers and procedure of the Supreme Court and provisions relating to appeals therefrom to His Majesty in Council and provisions relating to the qualifications, order of precedence, duties and powers of Judges and appointment of temporary Judges, may, subject to the provisions of this Order, be prescribed by laws made by the Legislature of the Colony or enacted by the Governor under Part V of this Order.

(4) Nothing in this section shall be taken to prejudice the right of His Majesty upon the humble petition of any person aggrieved by any decision of the Court of Appeal to admit his appeal therefrom upon such conditions as His Majesty in Council shall think fit to impose.

===== Page 490 =====
or any part of such sentence or of forfeitures otherwise due to His Majesty.

13. (1) There shall be in and for the Union a Court of unlimited civil and criminal jurisdiction to be called the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court shall be a Court of Record and may consist of a High Court and a Court of Appeal.

(2) The constitution, powers and procedure of the Supreme Court and provisions relating to appeals therefrom to His Majesty in Council may, subject to the provisions of this Order, be prescribed by laws made by the legislature of the Union or enacted by the Governor under Part VIII of this Order.

(3) Nothing in this section shall be taken to prejudice the right of His Majesty upon the humble petition of any person aggrieved by any decision of the Court of Appeal to admit his appeal therefrom upon such conditions as His Majesty in Council shall think fit to impose.

14. (1) Courts subordinate to the Supreme Court and Courts of special jurisdiction may be constituted by or under the provisions of any Ordinance as occasion requires.

(2) Provision may be made by Ordinance for the hearing and determining of appeals from any such Court by the Supreme Court or otherwise.

===== Page 491 =====
15. There shall be an Executive Council for the Union, and the said Council shall consist of such persons as His Majesty shall direct by Instructions under His Sign Manual and Signet or through a Secretary of State, and all such persons shall hold their places in the Executive Council during His Majesty's pleasure and, subject thereto, for such period and upon such conditions as may be specified in any such instructions.

===== Page 492 =====
LEGISLATIVE COUNCIL

16. (1) There shall be a Legislative Council in and for the Colony, constituted in accordance with the provisions of this Order.

(2) The Council shall consist of the Governor as President, three ex officio Members, eighteen Nominated Official Members, and twenty-one Nominated Unofficial Members.

17. The ex officio Members shall be the Chief Secretary, the Attorney General, and the Financial Secretary.

18. The Nominated Official Members shall be the two official Members of the Executive Council other than the ex officio Members and sixteen other persons holding office of emolument under the Crown in the Union, and shall be appointed by the Governor by an Instrument under the Public Seal in pursuance of His Majesty's instructions through a Secretary of State.

19. (1) Save as hereinafter provided, the Nominated Unofficial Members shall be Malayan Union citizens of the age of twenty-one years or upwards not holding office of emolument under the Crown in the Union, who are able to speak and (unless incapacitated by blindness or other physical cause) to read and write the English language with a degree of proficiency sufficient to enable them to take an active part in the proceedings of the Council. Nominated Unofficial Members shall be appointed by the Governor by an Instrument under the Public Seal in pursuance of His Majesty's Instructions through a Secretary of State:

(2) In special cases, the Governor may, for cause shown to his satisfaction, appoint a person who is not a Malayan Union citizen to be a Nominated Unofficial Member.

===== Page 493 =====
Member of the Legislative Council.

20. No person shall be capable of being appointed an Unofficial Member of the Legislative Council or, having been appointed, shall sit and vote therein, who at the time of appointment -

(1) is an undischarged bankrupt; or

(2) has, in any part of His Majesty's dominions or in any territory under His protection, been sentenced to death (or penal servitude) or to imprisonment for a term exceeding twelve months and has not either suffered the punishment to which he was sentenced or such other punishment as may by competent authority have been substituted therefor or received a free pardon; or

(3) is a party to, or a member of a firm or a director or manager of a company which is a party to, or is interested in, any subsisting contract with the Government of the Union or with the Government of any State or Settlement therein for or on account of the public service and shall not have disclosed to the Governor the nature of such contract and his interest therein; or

(4) is a person found or declared insane under any law in force in the Union.

21. (1) Subject to the provisions of this Order, every Nominated Official Member or Nominated Unofficial Member of the Legislative Council shall hold his seat in the Legislative Council during His Majesty's pleasure and, subject thereto, every Nominated Official Member or Nominated Unofficial Member shall cease to be a Member at the end of three years from the date of the Instrument by which he is appointed or at such earlier date as may be provided by such Instrument; and every Member shall in any case cease to be a Member at the next dissolution of the Legislative Council after his appointment or election or previously if his seat shall become vacant under the provisions of this Order.

(2) The seat of a Member of the Legislative Council (other than an ex officio Member) shall become vacant -

===== Page 494 =====
(a) upon his death; or

(b) if he shall, without the leave of the Governor, be absent from two consecutive meetings of the Council;

(c) if he shall cease to be a Malayan Union citizen; or shall take any oath, or make any declaration or acknowledgment of allegiance, obedience or adherence to any foreign Power or State; or shall do, concur in or adopt any act done with the intention that he shall become a subject or citizen of any foreign Power or State; or

(d) if he shall be declared a bankrupt under any law in force in any part of His Majesty's dominions or in any territory under His Majesty's protection; or

(e) if in any part of His Majesty's dominions or in any territory under His Majesty's protection, he shall be sentenced to death or imprisonment by whatever name called for a term exceeding six months; or

(f) if, being Nominated Unofficial Member, he shall, without the approval of the Governor, become a party to, or if any firm in which he is a partner, or any company of which he is a director or manager, shall become a party to, or if he shall be otherwise interested in, any contract with the Government of the Union or of any State included in the Union for or on account of the public service and shall not have disclosed to the Governor the nature of such contract and his interest therein; or

(g) if he shall be found or declared insane under any law in force in the Union; or

(h) if being a Nominated Unofficial Member, he shall, by writing under his hand addressed to the Governor, resign his seat in the Legislative Council; or

(i) if, being an Official Member, he shall cease to hold office of emolument under the Crown in the Union; or

(j) if, being a Nominated Unofficial Member, he shall be appointed permanently to any such office;

(k) if he shall otherwise cease to possess qualification for appointment under the provisions of this Order.

(3) If a Nominated Unofficial Member of the Legislative Council shall be appointed temporarily to any office of emolument under the Crown in the Union, or shall be appointed to act in any such office, he shall not sit or vote in the Legislative Council so long as he continues to hold or to act in that office.

===== Page 495 =====
(4) Any person vacating a seat as a Member of the Legislative Council may, if qualified, be again appointed or elected as a member from time to time.

(5) The Governor may, by an Instrument under the Public Seal, declare any Nominated Official Member or Nominated Unofficial Member to be incapable of discharging his functions as a Member of the Council, and thereupon such Member shall not sit or vote in the Council until he is declared, in manner aforesaid, to be again capable of discharging his said functions.

(6) The Governor may, by an Instrument under the Public Seal, suspend any Nominated Official Member or Nominated Unofficial Member from the exercise of his functions as a Member of the Legislative Council. Every such suspension shall forthwith be reported by the Governor to His Majesty through a Secretary of State, and shall remain in force unless and until it shall be removed by the Governor by Instrument under the Public Seal or by His Majesty through a Secretary of State, or the person suspended ceases to be a Member of the Legislative Council.

(7) Subject to the provisions of this Order, all questions which may arise as to the right of any person to be or remain a Nominated Member shall be referred to and decided by the Governor in Council.

22. Whenever the seat of a Nominated Member becomes vacant, the vacancy shall be filled by appointment by the Governor.

23. Whenever there shall be a vacancy in the number of persons sitting in the Legislative Council as ex officio Members, or Nominated Official Members or Nominated Unofficial Members, by reason of the fact that

(a) an ex officio Member is administering the Government of the Union; or

(b) one person is lawfully discharging the functions of more than one of the three officers specified in section 17 of this Order; or

===== Page 496 =====
(c) no person is lawfully discharging the functions of one of those officers; or

(d) a Nominated Official Member is lawfully discharging the functions of one of those officers; or

(e) a Nominated Official Member or Nominated Unofficial Member is suspended from the exercise of his functions as a Member; or

(f) the seat of a Nominated Official Member or of a Nominated Unofficial Member is vacant for any cause other than a dissolution of the Council; or

(g) a Member is unable to sit or vote in the Council in consequence of a declaration by the Governor, as provided in this Order, that he is incapable of discharging his functions as a Member; or

(h) a Member is absent from the Union; or

(i) a Nominated Unofficial Member is unable to sit or vote in the Council in consequence of his having been appointed temporarily to an office of emolument under the Crown in the Union or to act in any such office;

the Governor may, by Instrument under the Public Seal, appoint a person to be a Temporary Member for the period of such vacancy.

(2) If the vacancy is in the number of persons sitting in the Council as ex officio Members or Nominated Official Members, the person appointed shall be a person qualified for appointment as a Nominated Official Member; and if the vacancy is in the number of persons sitting in the Council as Nominated Unofficial Members, the person appointed shall be a person qualified for appointment as a Nominated Unofficial Member.

(3) Every person appointed to be a Temporary Member under this section shall, as long as his appointment shall subsist, be to all intents and purposes -

(a) in the case of a vacancy in the number of persons sitting in the Council as ex officio Members or Nominated Official Members, a Nominated Official Member;

(b) in the case of a vacancy in the number of persons sitting in the Council as Nominated Unofficial Members, a Nominated Unofficial Member.

===== Page 497 =====
And, subject to the provisions of this section, the provisions of the last preceding section of this Order shall apply accordingly:

Provided that, in the case of a vacancy in the number of persons sitting in the Council as ex officio Members, the person so appointed shall not be regarded as a Nominated Official Member in computing the number of such Members for the purposes of sub-section (2) of section 16 of this Order.

(4) The Governor shall forthwith report every such temporary appointment to His Majesty through a Secretary of State. Any such appointment may (without prejudice to anything done by virtue thereof) be disallowed by His Majesty through a Secretary of State or revoked by the Governor by Instrument under the Public Seal.

(5) A temporary appointment under this section shall cease to have effect on notification by the Governor to the person appointed of disallowance by His Majesty or of revocation by the Governor or on supersession of the appointment by the definitive appointment of a person to fill the vacancy, or when the vacancy shall otherwise cease to exist.

24. The Governor may summon to any meeting or session of the Legislative Council any person holding public office under the Crown in the Union, notwithstanding that such person may not be a Member of the Council, when, in the opinion of the Governor or such Member, the business before the Council renders the presence of such person desirable. Any person so summoned shall be entitled to take part in the proceedings of the Council relating to the business in respect of which he is summoned as if he were a Member of the Council, except that he shall not have the right to vote in the Council.

25. The Governor, if present, shall preside at meetings of the Council, and, in his absence, such Member as the

===== Page 498 =====
Governor may appoint or, in the absence of such Member, or if no Member be so appointed, the senior Member of Council actually present shall preside.

26. The Council shall not be disqualified for the transaction of business by reason of any vacancy among the Members; but no business except that of adjournment shall be transacted if objection is taken by any Member present that there are less than 1/4 Members present besides the Governor or other Member presiding.

27. The Members of the Council shall take precedence as His Majesty may specially assign and, in default thereof, as follows:

First, the ex officio Members in the order in which they are mentioned in section 17 of this Order;

Secondly, the Nominated Official Members, according to the priority of their appointments as Nominated Official Members, or, if appointed on the same day, in the order assigned by the Governor;

Thirdly, Nominated Official Members temporarily appointed under section 23 of this Order, according to the priority of their appointments or, if appointed on the same day, in the order assigned by the Governor;

Fourthly, the remaining Members according to the length of time during which they have been continuously Members of the Council: Members elected or appointed on the same day taking precedence among themselves in such order as the Governor may direct:

Provided that, when a Member is re-appointed to fill a vacancy caused by the termination of his own term of office or by dissolution of the Council, the date from which he has been continuously a Member of the Council (any interval between such termination and re-appointment being disregarded) shall be regarded as the date of his appointment for the purpose of this section.

===== Page 499 =====
PART IV.

LEGISLATION AND PROCEDURE IN LEGISLATIVE COUNCIL.

28. Subject to the provisions of this Order, it shall be lawful for the Governor, with the advice and consent of the Legislative Council, to make laws for the peace, order, and good government of the Union.

29. Save as otherwise provided in this Order, all questions proposed for decision in the Council shall be determined by a majority of the votes of the Members present and voting. The Governor shall not have an original vote, but, if upon any question the votes shall be equally divided, he shall have a casting vote. In the absence of the Governor, the Member presiding shall have an original vote and also, if upon any question the votes shall be equally divided, a casting vote.

30. (1) If the Governor shall consider that it is expedient in the interests of public order, public faith, or good government that any Bill introduced, or any motion proposed for decision in the Council shall have effect, then if the Council fail to pass such a Bill or motion within such time and in such form as the Governor may think reasonable and expedient, the Governor, at any time in his discretion, may, notwithstanding any provisions of this Order or of any Standing Rules or Orders of the Council, declare that such Bill or motion shall have effect as if it had been passed by the Council, either in the form in which it was so introduced or proposed or with such amendments as the Governor shall think fit which have been moved or proposed in the Council or in any Committee thereof; and thereupon the said Bill or motion shall have effect as if it has been so passed, and, in the case of any such Bill, the provisions of this Order relating to assent to Bills and disallowance of laws shall apply.

===== Page 500 =====
